grader_proofs_verified: Furthermore, the results showed that montmorillonite adsorbed more Cs than illite and kaolinite under all tested conditions.
grader_why: The shown sentence states directly that montmorillonite adsorbed more Cs than both illite and kaolinite under all tested conditions, which establishes both its high (superior) adsorption capacity and its outperformance of the two named minerals. Both components of the claim are proven by the displayed sentence itself. No unsourced or contradicted subclaim is present.
fable_reasoning: Comparative claim proven verbatim; the uncovered 'high adsorption capacity' is trivially entailed by outperforming two other clays.

grader_components.part: zeolite shows the highest overall sorption among minerals
weathered biotite appears to be the best clay sorption material

grader_missing_subclaim: weathered biotite appears to be the best clay sorption material
grader_rewrite_suggestion: While zeolite shows the highest overall sorption among the minerals studied, glauconite appears to be the best argillaceous (clay) sorption material.
grader_proofs_verified: Sorption values decrease as follows: zeolite glauconite >bentonite >diatomite (Table 5).
The highest cesium sorption value was observed on zeolite, due to its high CEC (161 mg eq /100 g).

The higher sorption value on glauconite in comparison with bentonite is primarily caused by the high layer charge which is mainly localized in tetrahedral sheets and also with the existence of highly selective sorption centers ( Frayed Edge Sites ) on the glauconite surface, and competition with interlayer sodium in montmorillonite, especially at low pH values.
grader_why: The source fully supports that zeolite has the highest overall sorption (Table 5 and the conclusions state zeolite is highest, with glauconite the best-performing clay/argillaceous mineral). However, the source never mentions biotite anywhere; the studied clay minerals are glauconite, bentonite, montmorillonite, illite, smectite, and kaolinite, and glauconite—not biotite—is identified as the best argillaceous sorbent. The 'weathered biotite is the best clay sorption material' subclaim is not provable from this source at all, so the author must add a citation or rewrite.
fable_reasoning: CALIBRATION SIGNAL: pipeline flat unsupported, but 'zeolite shows the highest overall sorption' is proven verbatim (zeolite >= glauconite > bentonite > diatomite). Only 'weathered biotite is the best clay material' is unsupported. Partial, not unsupported.

grader_components.part: natural Ca-bentonite higher capacity than Na-bentonite
inorganic modified Na-bentonite high adsorption under alkaline conditions
these specific bentonite types contain montmorillonite

grader_missing_subclaim: that the Kunipia-F bentonite and the natural Ca-/Na-bentonites contain montmorillonite — only the inorganic-modified-bentonite source states montmorillonite content; the Kunipia-F source and the Ca/Na-bentonite source never state it in the provided text
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Specific bentonite types are effective adsorbents: Kunipia-F bentonite is superior to SiO2, natural Ca-bentonite has a higher capacity than Na-bentonite, and inorganic modified Na-bentonite shows high adsorption under alkaline conditions.
grader_proofs_verified: It was found that the Cs adsorption capacity of bentonite was higher than that of SiO 2 .

The results indicate that adsorption could be completed within 12 h, the optimal solid–liquid ratio was 20 g/L, a weakly alkaline environment was more suitable for adsorption, and the maximum adsorption capacity of Ca-bentonite measured at 114.5 mg/g, while Na-bentonite was 92.3 mg/g.

The adsorption effect of Na-Bentonite and K-Bentonite under alkaline conditions is better than that of unmodified Bentonite, while in acidic situation, the K-Bentonite and Mg-Bentonite present poor adsorption performance with the unit adsorption amount lower than 0.5 mg/g.
As shown in Fig. 9a), the components before and after adsorption is almost no difference, which mostly composed of montmorillonite and quartz.
grader_why: All three comparative subclaims are individually proven by the shown sentences (Kunipia-F > SiO2; Ca-bentonite 114.5 > Na-bentonite 92.3 mg/g; modified Na-bentonite better under alkaline conditions). However, the aside 'which contain montmorillonite' is not in any shown sentence and is only establishable for the inorganic-modified-bentonite source; the Kunipia-F and Ca/Na-bentonite sources never state their materials' montmorillonite content in the provided text. Because that component is not provable from the sources it is attributed to, the author must drop or separately cite it (mixed case → author fix).
fable_reasoning: CALIBRATION SIGNAL (same pattern as paper1 t23/t24/t30/t32): pipeline flat unsupported, but the arbiter proof supports the 'Kunipia-F bentonite superior to SiO2' component. The other components (contains montmorillonite; Ca- vs Na-bentonite; inorganic-modified) are unshown. Multi-component claim with one proven part = partial, not unsupported.
